mappings.rationaleAI: Biochemical principles of human nutrition covering macronutrients, vitamins, and metabolic processes directly address essential nutrients and nutritional needs (A.3) and the digestive process (C).
Science of diet, nutrition, and food preferences addresses nutritional patterns (A), essential nutrients (A.3), and digestive processing of food (C).
Plant photosynthetic capacity addresses autotrophism as a nutritional pattern (A.1) and photosynthesis as the production of food in green plants (B).
Energy supply and bioenergetics address nutritional needs (A.3), and physiological interplay covers vertebrate digestive system dynamics and control of secretions (C.2.d).
Organs of the chest and abdomen including structures for swallowing relate to the vertebrate digestive system structures (C.2.b).
Animal diversity across phyla reveals varied digestive systems from invertebrate vacuolar systems (C.2.a) through vertebrate digestive structures (C.2.b).
Enzymatic catalysis central to digestion directly addresses biodynamics of the vertebrate digestive system (C.2.d).
Dental morphology linking tooth form to feeding ecology addresses methods of ingestion (A.2) and vertebrate oral cavity and teeth structures (C.2.b).
Insect-microbe nutritional partnerships and plant root associations exemplify syntrophism (A.4).
Microbial communities promoting healthy growth through nutritional support exemplify syntrophic nutritional interrelationships (A.4).
Microbial ecology including nitrogen fixation illustrates diverse nutritional patterns such as chemotrophism (A.1) and syntrophic relationships (A.4).
Crop cultivation connects to nutritional patterns (A) and photosynthetic food production by plants (B).
